coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of catecholamines using NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ upconversion nanoparticles decorated with metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Gerelkhuu, Zayakhuu
Jung, Dasom
The Huy, Bui
Tawfik, Salah M.
Conte, Maxwell L.
Conte, Eric D.
Lee, Yong-Ill
coredata.dc:description: We developed a novel optical sensor for sensitive and selective detection of catecholamines based on upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) decorated with different metal ions (UCNP-Men+). 1,2-ethanedithiol was chosen as a surface additive to synthesize the NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs by one-step at 200 °C. The as-prepared UCNPs exhibited a strong emission under the continuous excitation at 980 nm. It was found that catecholamines could be more effectively detected in the presence of UCNP-Fe3+, whereas, dopamine and epinephrine were detected selectively using UCNP-Li+ and UCNP-Cu2+ sensors, respectively. Under the optimum conditions, the limit of detections (LODs) for catecholamines, dopamine, and epinephrine are 2.8, 2.5, and 2.4 nM, respectively, with good linearity in the range of 5–320 nM for total catecholamines and 5–30 nM for dopamine and epinephrine. The developed method has been successfully applied to detect dopamine and epinephrine in human urine samples with good accuracy and satisfactory recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Catecholamines
Metal ion decorated
Upconversion nanoparticles
1,2-ethanedithiol
Human urine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of catecholamines in human urine sample
Luminescence properties of thiol-coated NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs
Detection of DA and EP in human urine samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program though the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2017R1A2B4006388 ) and OISE IRES program of National Science Foundation (No. 1358222 ) in USA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern (b) FT-IR spectrum, (c) FE-SEM image, and (d) FE-TEM image of thiol-coated NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs.
(a) The UCL spectrum of NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs and UV–vis absorption spectra of CAs decorated with metal ions. (b) Photographs of CAs (DA/EP/NEP) decorated with metal ions.

Selective detection of CAs using Fe3+: (a) UCL spectra of the UCNP-Fe3+ with total CAs and common possible interferences, (b) Relative UCL intensities at 542 nm of the evaluation systems recorded in correspondence with the UCL spectra in Fig. 3a, (c) UCL spectra of UCNP-Fe3+ with different concentration of CAs, and (d) the corresponding Stern-Volmer plot (inset: expanded linear region of the calibration curve).
UCL spectra of (a) UCNP-Li+ and (b) UCNP-Cu2+ with DA, EP, and NEP.

UCL spectra of (a) UCNP-Li+ with different concentration of DA, (b) the corresponding Stern-Volmer plot (inset: expanded linear region of the calibration curve), (c) UCNP-Cu2+ with different concentration of EP, and (d) the corresponding Stern-Volmer plot (inset: expanded linear region of the calibration curve).
Schematic representation for selective and sensitive detection of CAs with UCNPs decorated with different metal ions.
The comparison of the detection limit of DA and EP by different sensing probes.
Determination of DA and EP in human urine sample.

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of catecholamines using NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ upconversion nanoparticles decorated with metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changwon National University, Changwon 51140, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Western Kentucky University, 1906 College Heights Blvd., Bowling Green 42101, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We developed a novel optical sensor for sensitive and selective detection of catecholamines based on upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) decorated with different metal ions (UCNP-Men+). 1,2-ethanedithiol was chosen as a surface additive to synthesize the NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs by one-step at 200 °C. The as-prepared UCNPs exhibited a strong emission under the continuous excitation at 980 nm. It was found that catecholamines could be more effectively detected in the presence of UCNP-Fe3+, whereas, dopamine and epinephrine were detected selectively using UCNP-Li+ and UCNP-Cu2+ sensors, respectively. Under the optimum conditions, the limit of detections (LODs) for catecholamines, dopamine, and epinephrine are 2.8, 2.5, and 2.4 nM, respectively, with good linearity in the range of 5–320 nM for total catecholamines and 5–30 nM for dopamine and epinephrine. The developed method has been successfully applied to detect dopamine and epinephrine in human urine samples with good accuracy and satisfactory recovery.

ce:section.ce:para: Catecholamines (CAs) such as dopamine (DA), epinephrine (EP), and norepinephrine (NEP) play vital roles as neurotransmitters or hormones in central and peripheral nervous systems [1]. Dysfunctions of the CAs systems may lead to several nervous system diseases such as Parkinson’s disease and Schizophrenia [2–4]. DA is derived from L-tyrosine by a series of biochemical reactions using tyrosine hydroxylase and decarboxylase, and it is the precursor to EP and NEP [5]. DA presented in mammalian nigrostriatal neurons are known to mediate motor control, respiration, blood pressure, intuition, focus, and drug addiction [6–8]. A hallmark of Parkinson’s disease is the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the brain. Drugs including cocaine act by modulating the transport of dopamine into the neurons. NEP is another essential neurotransmitter and is known to mediate excitation of the heart and smooth muscles, decrease peripheral circulation, stimulate arteriole contraction, and affect the human cardiovascular system [9]. It is also associated with neurological diseases such as depression and obsessive-compulsive disorder [10]. Another neurotransmitter, EP (adrenalin) is typically produced by both the adrenal glands and certain neurons [11]. EP plays an important role in the fight-or-flight response by increasing blood flow into muscles with rising heart rate and blood sugar. Changes in the concentration of EP may indicate diseases related to Huntington’s, drug addiction and HIV infection [12,13]. Therefore, highly selective and sensitive sensor to determine and continuously sense changes in the CAs levels in biological samples is of great importance.

During the past decade, a number of analytical techniques for the advanced detection and quantification of CAs have been developed by using electrochemistry [14,15], colorimetry [16–19], fluorescence spectroscopy [20–23], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [24], and a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) [25,26]. Several fluorescence probes also have been proposed using quantum dots (QDs) [27], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [28], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [29], and carbon dots (CDs) [20]. However, the above mentioned works had been conducted for sensitive detection of individual CAs in various matrices including biological fluids.

In previous studies, Doong’s group reported that DA and Fe3+ ion has a specific interaction, whereby the catechol group of DA coordinates with Fe3+ ion and is then oxidized to o-semiquinone [30]. It was also provided that DA bind to Fe3+ to from colorful Fe(DA)n (n = 1–3) complexes [17]. Those works demonstrated a colorimetric detection of DA only without any differentiation from EP and NEP because Fe3+ ion can oxidize all compounds which contain catechol groups [31].

Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs), which can convert near-infrared (NIR) radiation to visible fluorescence, are emerging as a new generation of fluorescence bioimaging and optosensing [32]. With NIR excitation, the signal-to-noise ratio is markedly enhanced, compared with UV excitation, owing to the absence of both auto-fluorescence and scattered light of biological samples themselves. Additionally, low toxicity and high degree of biocompatibility of living cells and organisms further make UCNPs an attractive platform for biological and medicinal applications [33–35]. To date, UCNPs have been used as an energy donor in conjunction with various acceptors including gold nanoparticles [36], graphene oxide [37], organic dyes [38], and MnO2 nanosheets [39] based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). To the best of our knowledge, there is no report using UCNPs for the selective detection of CAs in biological samples. This is most likely due to the difficulty to find appropriate functional groups of UCNPs that can selectively sense CAs compounds.

In this work, we developed a new method for detecting CAs selectively based on the quenching effect of the upconversion luminescence (UCL) emission of NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs. The highly selective detection of each of the CAs was accomplished through the sequential reactions of CAs with different metal ions. UCNPs decorated with the ferric (Fe3+) ion (UCNP-Fe3+) were used to detect the total amount of CAs presented in the first step of our analytical procedure. Then Cu2+ and Li+ ions were introduced as “triggers” for the specific detection of EP and DA, respectively (Scheme 1 ). The present method was successfully applied for the detection of DA and EP in human urine sample.

In a typical experiment, NaCl (2.4 mM), RECl3 (1 mM, Lu:Gd:Yb:Er = 68:12:18:2), and 1,2-etanedithiol (EDT, 7 mM) was dissolved in 15 mL of ethylene glycol (EG), then 5 mL of NH4F (5 mM) was added with stirring. After 30 min, the mixture was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined container and transferred to an autoclave and subsequently heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, precipitates were separated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water several times, and then dried under vacuum at 55 °C for 12 h. The UCNPs were redispersed in a PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4), with a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL as the stock solution of UCNPs.

To investigate the UCL quenching effect, 7 mM of Fe3+/Cu2+/Li+ solutions were prepared in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and were added into the solution of UCNPs (1 mM). The solution was then shaken for 2 h at room temperature. Different concentrations of CAs, DA, EP (0.005–100 μM) were mixed with UCNPs-Fe3+/Cu2+/Li+ solution with the total volume being 3 mL. The sample solution were gently stirred and incubated for 60 min at room temperature before measuring UCL spectra with excitation at 980 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of catecholamines in human urine sample
ce:section.ce:para: The treatment of urine sample was performed according to the procedure in our previous report [40]. Briefly, the urine sample was collected from healthy volunteers and mixed with acetonitrile (ratios of 1:1, v/v) to remove proteins. Then the solution was vortex-mixed and allowed to stand for 3 h at 10 °C. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and diluted 50 times with distilled water. Spiked samples were prepared by adding different concentrations of DA or EP (6, 12, and 18 nM) and UCNP-Men+ to the diluted urine samples. The as-prepared samples were exposed to 980 nm laser diode (LD) before the UCL spectra were collected after 60 min. All the experiments were repeated four times and performed at room temperature.

Crystal structures of the developed UCNPs were investigated by using an X-ray diffractometer, X’Pert PW3040/00, from PANalytical (Almelo, Netherlands) using Cu Kα (ratio Kα2/Kα1 = 0.5) radiation. Scans of selected diffraction peaks were carried out in step mode (step size 0.02°) at 25 °C. The morphology of the UCNPs was determined by Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscope (FE-TEM; JEM 2100 F, Japan) and Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM; CZ/MIRA I LMH, Tescan, Czech). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) emission spectra were measured using an EDAX X-ray detector located inside a FE-SEM (CZ/MIRA I LMH, Tescan, Czech). The electron beam was accelerated at 20 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by using a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Madison, USA). The UCL emission spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Acton Spectra Pro 750-Triplet Grating Monochromator) with CCD detection (Princeton EEV 1024 × 1024 and PI-Max 133 Controller), using a 980 nm LD as an excitation source, which was placed at a 45-degree angle in front of the sample holder.

UCNPs coated with EDT were prepared using an one-step solvothermal method. EDT contains dithiol groups with the formula C2H4(SH)2. One -SH group binds to the surface of the UCNPs and the other -SH group is free. This free group improves the water solubility of UCNPs and can complex with metal ions.

The crystal structure of NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs was confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 1 a). All peaks in the XRD pattern were well indexed to the reference hexagonal phase of NaLuF4 (JCPDS-27-0726), and no other impurity peaks were detected.

The thiol-coated NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs are identified by FT-IR spectra, as shown in Fig. 1b. The broad absorption band around 3458 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH. The FT-IR peaks at 1421 and 2930 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CH and CH2 bond, respectively. The peak at 2538 cm−1 is due to SH stretching of thiol moiety. However, the intensity of this peak is weak (SH has a weak dipole) as reported in the literature [41].

FE-SEM and FE-TEM were used to observe the morphology and size of the prepared thiol-coated NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs. As shown in Fig. 1c & d, the particles have an average size of 100 nm and are uniform. Elemental analysis was carried out to prove further incorporation of S (thiol groups) successfully in the NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs. The EDS spectrum in Fig. S1 (in Supporting Information) confirmed the presence of Na, Gd, Lu, F, Yb, Er and S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence properties of thiol-coated NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The UCL spectrum of thiol-coated NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs was recorded by using a spectrophotometer with an excitation at 980 nm of LD, as shown in Fig. S2. Strong three-featured emission bands centered at 522, 542 and 655 nm are observed, corresponding to the 2H11/2 → 4I15/2, 4S3/2 → 4I15/2, and 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 transitions of Er3+, respectively. The UCL intensity and size of thiol-coated NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs were highly reproducible within RSD < 2% for four times duplications of the synthetic process.

The energy level diagram of Yb3+ and Er3+, and the possible UC processes are illustrated in Fig. S3 [32,42]. Near-infrared radiation (980 nm) is absorbed by Yb3+ (2F7/2 → 2F5/2) and transferred to Er3+, resulting in the increase of electron population at the 4I11/2 level of Er3+. The Er3+ ion is then raised from the 4I11/2 to the 4F7/2 level. The excited electron decays to 2H11/2, 4S3/2, and 4F9/2 levels through non-radiative relaxation process and then decays further to the ground state with the emissions at 522, 542, and 655 nm.

Our strategy for developing a new optosensing method for selective detection of CAs with sequential separation using different metal ions as “triggers” of UCNPs was designed, as illustrated in Scheme 1. Lebedev’s group reported that oxygen readily oxidizes catechol groups in a given molecule. These oxidations occur in basic solutions or by transition metals. Readily found interactions between catechol groups and metal ions (Men+) produces catecholate, semiquinone, or quinone forms [31]. Furthermore, the Fe3+ and catechol group of dopamine have a specific interaction in the human body and is found in Parkinson’s disease patients [43]. The detailed mechanism of the Fe(DA)2 complex was investigated in the pH range of 5.7–7.2. It has been reported that Fe3+ induces the aggregation of UCNPs by coordinating to DA at pH above 5.7 [17,44].

To evaluate our concept, we first performed the detection of all CAs (DA, EP, and NEP) in aqueous solution using photoluminescence upon the formation of complexes between CAs and UCNPs decorated with different metal ions. As observed in Fig. 2 a, UCNPs give strong green emission bands centered at 522 (2H11/2 → 4I15/2) and 542 (4S3/2 → 4I15/2), and a weak red band centered at 655 nm (4F9/2 → 4I15/2) originating from Er3+ by the excitation of 980 nm LD. The complexes of EP-Fe3+ and EP-Cu2+ produce a new absorption peak around 500 nm, whereas a broad absorption peak from 400 to 600 nm was found with those of DA-Fe3+, NEP-Fe3+, and DA-Li+. Different colors were appeared depending on the specific metal ion/analyte complex formed (Fig. 2b), which show the potential for a selective analytical tool for these CAs substances.

In comparison, UV–vis absorption spectra of CAs did not reveal any distinct absorption peaks in the range of 400–600 nm (Fig. S4a). Moreover, we found that the UCL intensity of UCNPs without metal decoration was little influenced by 1 mM of CAs (DA, EP, and NEP) (Fig. S4b). These results implied that CAs could not be detected using UCNPs without metal decoration. The UCL intensities of NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs towards Fe3+ changed negligibly with increasing the concentrations of Fe3+ (from 5 mM to 9 mM), inducing the complex formation of the thiol-coated UCNPs with Fe3+ [45], as shown in Fig. S5a. Also, Cu2+ and Li+ ions were not influenced to the UCL intensity, as displayed in Fig. S5b & c. The upconversion luminescence property of UCNP-Fe3+/Li+/Cu2+ revealed stable with high concentration of metal ions. Moreover, the time dependent availability of the proposed sensor was estimated to show that it was stable in 6 months when the developed sensor was kept in dark.

UCNP-Fe3+ is intended to be used for detecting the total amount of CAs. Therefore, we studied the possible UCL quenching effect of UCNPs caused by not only CAs but also other common interferences in biological samples. As shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, the UCL intensity of the UCNP-Fe3+ exhibited a significant decrease after adding DA to the solution, whereas other CAs (EP, and NEP) were not much affected. The DA, EP, and NEP have catechol groups which are easily oxidized by UCNP-Fe3+. Catechol groups of CAs are high reactivity of molecular oxygen towards iron (III)-catecholate complex. Therefore, the presence of CAs resulted in a violet color with UCNP-Men+ has been reported that catechol moiety of CAs coordinates with Fe3+ to form a hydroquinone which is a strong electron-acceptor [31,46]. Other possible interferences such as amino acids (including Tyr, His, Lys, and Phe) ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), glucose (Glu) did not cause apparent changes in UCL intensity.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the UCL intensity of UCNP-Fe3+ decreased gradually with increasing the concentration of total CAs. A linear relationship between (F0/F)-1 measured at 542 nm and the concentration of total CAs ranging from 5 nM to 320 nM has been constructed with a good linear correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.994), where, F0 is the UCL intensity of sample without analyte and F is the UCL intensity of sample with analyte. The limit of detection (LOD) of the total CAs was calculated to be 2.8 nM based on 3σ/m, where σ corresponds to the standard deviation of the blank measurements and m is the slope of the plot of the UCL intensity versus the sample concentrations, as shown in Fig. 3d. All the experiments were repeated four times and the value of RSD was 2.2%.

The reaction between DA, EP, and NEP with different metal ions such as Cu2+, Co2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Li+, Mo3+, and Sr2+ were studied, and shown in Fig. S6. Most metal ions did not react with DA, EP and NEP. However, the reaction between EP and Cu2+ leads to change the solution color from colorless to orange, corresponding to a new peak centered at 480 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Whereas, the reaction between DA and Li+ exhibited broad absorption spectrum, as shown in Fig. S7. Besides that, EP was also reacted to Hg+, and V5+ but these metal ions are not often existing in human body and possessed high toxicity, therefore these metal ions are excluded in this study. It is challenging to introduce Cu2+ and Li+ ions to detect CAs selectively in a controllable and efficient manner. We also found that the UCL intensities of UCNP-Cu2+ and UCNP-Li+ were quenched by EP and DA, respectively (Fig. 4 ). The quenching of UCL intensity originated from a FRET process which requires not only an overlap of the emission of UCNP (as a donor) and the absorption of DA-Li+/EP-Cu2+ (as an acceptor) fulfilled the FRET process, but also a short donor-acceptor distance with small ionic radius (0.55 Å for Fe3+, 0.73 Å for Cu2+, and 0.76 Å for Li+).

As shown in Fig. 5 , the UCL intensities of UCNP-Li+ and UCNP-Cu2+ decreased with increasing the concentrations of DA and EP, respectively. The relationship between UCL intensity and the concentrations of DA and EP exhibited a remarkably linear decrease within the range of 5–30 nM. The regression equation was determined to be (F0/F)-1 = 0.0128[DA] + 0.0224 with a correlation coefficient of 0.988 for DA; and (F0/F)-1 = 0.0068[EP] + 0.0101 with a correlation coefficient of 0.992 for EP (F0-UCL intensity of sample without analyte; F-UCL intensity of sample with analyte; [DA] and [EP] are the concentrations of DA and EP (nM), respectively). The LOD values based on 3σ/m were calculated to be 2.8, 2.5, and 2.4 nM for total CAs, DA, and EP, respectively. These results are much better than those of other sensing probes in the literature, as summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA and EP in human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the reliability and applicability of the developed UCNP-Li+ and UCNP-Cu2+ sensors, they were employed for the analysis of DA and EP in the human urine samples. The results are displayed in Table 2 . The recoveries of DA and EP were found to be 92–105 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 5%. These values demonstrate the high accuracy and the high potential applicability of our sensor for analyzing CAs in real samples.

We have developed a novel optical sensor for detecting CAs selectively based on the UCL quenching effect of thiol-coated NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs decorated with metal ions, which were synthesized via one-step solvothermal method. Fe3+, Cu2+, and Li+ ions were used for decorating the thiol-coated UCNPs for the highly selective detection of each of the CAs through the sequential reactions of CAs. The UCL quenching intensities were used for calculating the total amount of CAs with UCNP-Fe3+, DA with UCNP- Li+, and EP with UCNP- Cu2+, respectively. The LOD values of the total CAs, DA, EP determined by UCNPs decorated with metal ions were 2.8, 2.5, and 2.4 nM, respectively. Our developed method presents an excellent analytical performance including good linearity in the range of 5–320 nM for total CAs and 5–30 nM for DA and EP, and good recoveries of 92–105 %. The presented sensor was adapted for the detection of DA and EP in human urine samples with satisfactory results and gives a potential for practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program though the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1A2B4006388) and OISE IRES program of National Science Foundation (No. 1358222) in USA.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.135.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: EDS; UCL spectrum of NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs; UC energy transfer mechnism of UCNPs; UV–vis absorption spectra of DA, EP, and NEP; UCL spectra of NaLuGdF4:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs with DA, EP, and NEP; UCL spectra of UCNPs after loading of different concentrations of Fe3+, Cu2+, and Li+; UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu2+ and Li+ with DA, EP, and NEP.
The following are Supplementary data to this article:
